title: Speed Benchmarking
lesson:
Speed benchmarking means measuring, with real numbers, how fast a model actually serves answers — so you can compare two models, two providers, or two setups fairly instead of guessing. A vendor's blog might shout 'blazing fast!', but 'fast' is meaningless until you decide fast at what, under what load, and measured how. This topic teaches the handful of metrics that matter and how to gather them honestly.

The four numbers that matter

Speed is never one number. The same model can look snappy or sluggish depending on which clock you watch. The four you'll use most:

  • TTFT — time to first token: how long from hitting send until the first word appears. The 'is it frozen?' feeling.
  • TPOT — time per output token: once words flow, how fast each new one arrives. Its inverse is tokens per second (TPS) — the number quoted most.
  • Total latency: the whole wait for one request. Roughly TTFT + (TPOT × tokens generated).
  • Throughput: how many tokens (or requests) the whole system pushes out per second across all users at once.

Latency vs throughput: the motorway

Latency and throughput are the easiest pair to confuse, and the confusion is expensive. Think of a motorway. Latency is how long your car takes from one junction to the next. Throughput is how many cars pass a point per minute.

Add more lanes and total throughput soars — but if every lane is jammed, your personal trip is still slow. A server using batch inference behaves exactly like this: cramming many requests through the GPU together lifts throughput but can increase any single user's latency. Optimise for the wrong one and a 'fast' system feels terrible to the person waiting.

Averages lie — use percentiles

The biggest beginner mistake is reporting an average latency. Averages hide the bad moments. Picture a restaurant where 9 in 10 diners are served in 2 minutes but 1 in 10 waits 30 minutes — the 'average' looks fine while a tenth of your customers are furious.

So we use percentiles: p50 (the median — half of requests are faster than this) and p95 or p99 (95% or 99% are faster). The p99 is your tail latency — the worst-case wait real users actually hit. A good benchmark always reports p50 and p95/p99, never a bare average.

How to measure it honestly

To capture TTFT you must stream the response (receive tokens as they're produced) and timestamp the very first chunk. Here's the core of a real benchmark loop:

import time
start = time.perf_counter()
ttft = None
n_tokens = 0
for chunk in client.chat.completions.create(
        model="my-model", messages=msgs, stream=True):
    if ttft is None:
        ttft = time.perf_counter() - start   # first token landed
    n_tokens += 1
total = time.perf_counter() - start
tps = n_tokens / total
print(f"TTFT={ttft:.3f}s  total={total:.3f}s  {tps:.1f} tok/s")

Run that once and you have a single sample — almost worthless. Real benchmarking means running it many times across realistic conditions. Three rules keep you honest:

  1. Warm up first. The first calls are slow — caches are cold and the model may still be loading into VRAM. Throw away the first handful before counting.
  2. Fix the variables. Speed depends on prompt length, output length, and concurrency. Change one knob at a time, or you won't know what caused a difference.
  3. Test under realistic load. A model doing 80 tok/s for one lonely request might drop to 15 tok/s each when 50 users hit it together. Benchmark at the concurrency you actually expect.

Why the same model gives different numbers

It's normal — even correct — for the 'same' model to benchmark differently. The hardware (GPU), the serving engine (vLLM vs a basic loop), quantization level, batch size, and prompt length all move the numbers. Longer prompts make TTFT worse because the model must read them all before speaking. Tricks like speculative decoding and the KV cache raise TPS for nearly free.

So a benchmark is only meaningful with its setup attached. 'GPT-class model, 4-bit, vLLM, A100, 1k-token prompt, 8 concurrent' tells a story; '120 tok/s' alone tells you nothing.

💡 Speed never lives alone. Always read it next to cost-per-token and answer quality — a model that's twice as fast but wrong is not a win. That balancing act is the whole point of the latency vs quality tradeoff, and speed benchmarking is just how you put real numbers on the 'latency' half so the decision stops being a hunch.
mentalModel: Speed is a motorway, not a single speedometer. Latency is how long your one car takes between junctions; throughput is how many cars pass per minute. Adding lanes (batching) boosts throughput but can slow your personal trip — so measure both, report the worst-case (p99) not the average, and always attach the exact setup the numbers came from.
takeaways: Speed is four numbers: TTFT, tokens/sec, total latency, and throughput.
Report percentiles (p50, p95/p99) — averages hide the painful tail.
Always warm up, fix variables, test at real concurrency, attach the setup.

diagram.caption: One streamed response exposes four clocks: TTFT to the first token, TPOT across the stream, total latency for the whole request, and system-wide throughput across all users.

mistakes: Reporting an average instead of percentiles. Averages bury the painful tail. Half your users could be fine while the slowest 5% suffer 10-second waits, and the mean looks healthy. Always quote p50 and p95/p99 so the worst-case real users actually feel is visible.

Confusing latency with throughput. A server tuned for high throughput (many batched requests per second) can make any single user wait longer. They are different goals — decide which one your product needs before you 'optimise for speed'.

Forgetting to warm up. The first calls hit cold caches and a model still loading into VRAM, so they're abnormally slow. Counting them poisons your results. Fire a few throwaway requests first, then start measuring.

Benchmarking with one request when production has hundreds. Single-request numbers look great and lie completely. A model doing 80 tok/s solo might give each user 15 tok/s under 50-way concurrency. Test at the load you'll actually run.

Quoting a number with no setup attached. '120 tok/s' is meaningless without the GPU, quantization, serving engine, prompt length and concurrency. The same model swings wildly across setups, so a bare figure can't be compared or reproduced.

Ignoring TTFT because you only watch tokens-per-second. For a live chatbot, a slow first token feels frozen even if the stream is fast afterwards. High TPS with a 4-second TTFT is a bad user experience — measure and report the first-token clock too.
exercise:
Pick one model you can call (a cloud API or a local one via Ollama) and write a small streaming benchmark. For a fixed prompt, measure TTFT, total latency, and tokens per second using a loop like the one in the lesson. Throw away the first 3 'warm-up' runs, then collect 20 timed runs. Compute and print the p50 and p95 for each metric (sort the list and pick the value at the 50% and 95% position). Notice how far apart p50 and p95 are — that gap is your tail.

Stretch goal: Fire the same prompt at increasing concurrency (1, then 4, then 16 requests at once using async or threads) and plot how per-request TPS falls while total system throughput rises. You've just measured the latency-vs-throughput curve with your own hands.
solution: Expect p95 to sit noticeably above p50 — often 1.5–3× higher — because of network jitter, garbage-collection pauses, and other requests sharing the GPU; that spread is exactly why averages mislead. TTFT is usually a few hundred milliseconds for a small/local model and can climb with longer prompts, since the model must ingest the whole prompt before emitting token one. For the stretch goal, you'll see the classic pattern: at concurrency 1 each request enjoys the full GPU and gets the best per-request TPS; as you add concurrent requests, per-request TPS drops while total tokens/second across all requests climbs and then plateaus when the GPU saturates. That plateau is your hardware's real ceiling — and the whole trade you must navigate when sizing a deployment.
summary: Speed benchmarking turns 'feels fast' into real numbers. Track four things: TTFT (first-token wait), TPOT / tokens-per-second (stream speed), total latency (one request), and throughput (the whole system). Report percentiles (p50 and p95/p99), never bare averages, because the tail is what hurts users. Always warm up, fix your variables, test at realistic concurrency, and attach the full setup — the same model gives wildly different numbers across hardware, quantization, and load. Read speed alongside cost and quality, never alone.

keyTerms.def: How long from sending a request until the first word of the answer appears. The 'is it frozen?' clock; critical for live chat.
Time per output token, and its inverse — how many tokens the model produces each second once the stream starts. The 'reading speed' of the response.
The full wait for a single request, roughly TTFT plus (time-per-token × number of tokens generated).
How many tokens or requests the whole system serves per second across all users at once — distinct from one user's latency, and usually raised by batching.
A value that a given share of requests beat. p50 is the median; p99 is the near-worst case. Used instead of averages because averages hide the painful tail.
The slow worst-case requests (the p95/p99). Small in count but exactly the moments where real users feel the product is broken.
How many requests the system is handling at the same time. Higher concurrency lifts throughput but can slow each individual request.
Throwaway requests run before timing begins, so cold caches and model-loading delays don't poison the measured numbers.

quiz.q: Users complain a chatbot 'feels frozen' for a few seconds, even though it eventually streams text quickly. Which metric is most likely the problem?
options: TTFT (time to first token) is too high
The p50 latency is below the p95
quiz.explain: The 'frozen' feeling before any text appears is exactly TTFT. A fast stream afterwards (high TPS) does not fix a slow first token.

quiz.q: You batch many requests through one GPU and total tokens/second across all users goes up, but individual users complain it got slower. What happened?
options: You raised throughput but increased per-request latency
You lowered both throughput and latency
Batching always lowers latency, so this is a bug
quiz.explain: Batching is the classic latency-vs-throughput trade: cramming requests through together lifts system throughput but can make any single user wait longer.

quiz.q: A report says the model averages 1.8s latency, but support tickets keep mentioning 10-second waits. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The average is simply wrong and should be recomputed
Latency and throughput were swapped in the report
A slow tail (high p95/p99) is hidden by the average
The model needs more warm-up requests
quiz.explain: Averages bury the tail: most requests can be fast while the slowest 5% are very slow. Percentiles like p95/p99 surface the worst-case real users hit.

quiz.q: Which benchmark result is actually meaningful and reproducible?
options: '4-bit, vLLM on an A100, 1k-token prompt, 8 concurrent: p50 TTFT 240ms, 95 tok/s'
quiz.explain: A number only means something with its full setup attached — hardware, quantization, engine, prompt length, concurrency, and percentiles. The same model swings wildly across setups, so bare figures can't be compared.
